text: The surgeon performs an abdominal exploration, revealing no visible ascites or carcinosis within the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the preoperative tattoo marking on the proximal transverse colon, indicating the location for resection.
The surgeon identifies a ten-centimeter distal margin on the colon using a Hem-o-lok clip, marking the area for resection.
The surgeon initiates the ileocolic resection at the duodenum, employing a bottom-up lateral-to-medial dissection technique.
The surgeon is positioned between the patient's legs, preparing to continue the ileocolic resection procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the D-II segment of the duodenum and the head of the pancreas, revealing these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the right parietocolic gutter using an ultrasonic device, extending the dissection up to the liver.
The surgeon operates from the left side of the patient, taking down the hepatic flexure, which involves separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon ligates and sections the ileocolic and right branch of the middle colic vessels, focusing on the vascular approach during the ileocolic resection.
The surgeon transects the mesocolon and mesentery using an ultrasonic device, completing the dissection with precise cuts through the tissue.
The surgeon has dissected the distal and proximal margins of the ileocolic resection site, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
Following the removal of the specimen, the surgeon prepares to perform an anastomosis, connecting the remaining sections of the ileum and colon.
The surgeon transects the colon and terminal ileum using a sixty linear stapler, introduced through the right lower trochanter, while positioned between the patient's legs.
The completion of the right hemicolectomy is visually confirmed, with the surgical field showing the resected area where the colon and terminal ileum were previously transected.

The specimen is removed through a protected suprapubic incision. The pneumoperitoneum is restored, and the surgeon approximates the ileum and colon stumps in an antiperistaltic fashion without applying traction.
The surgeon excises the medial end of both staple lines and inserts a linear stapler through the anterotomies via the right lower trocar, firing the stapler to create the anastomosis.
The surgeon places a stay suture to provide traction on both the posterior and anterior walls of the anastomosis, ensuring proper alignment and stability during the ileocolic resection.
The surgeon closes the enterotomy using a sixty blue cartridge stapler, inserted through the left lower trocar, completing the anastomosis.
The surgeon approximates the colon and ileum without dividing them, aligning the two sections for anastomosis.
The surgeon creates two enterotomies at the site designated for the future anastomosis, preparing the bowel segments for connection.
The surgeon inserts the stapler into the anastomosis site and fires it, completing the closure of the enterotomy as previously described.
The surgeon uses a stapler to close the enterotomy transversely, simultaneously resecting the proximal and distal margins of the ileum and colon.
The surgeon checks the length of the anastomosis and ensures there is no mesenteric interposition posteriorly, confirming the alignment of the colon and ileum.
The surgeon completes the ileocolic resection by placing four crotch stitches at the distal end of the anastomosis to prevent traction, ensuring stability and alignment of the anastomotic site.
The final result of the ileocolic resection is shown, with the mesentery appearing untwisted and without any visible traction.
